methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Defense & Security (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 21 same-sector peers exist in Defense & Security, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use

data.narrative:
Rebellion Defense is a Defense & Security company at SPAC stage that has raised $250M+. The broader Defense & Security sector comprises 21 peers, including 2 public comparables (DRSHF, IDEAFORGE.NS) and 8 private companies. Notable private peers include Anduril Industries, Scale AI, Saronic, Flock Safety, and Shield AI.

Standard peer-median valuation is not applicable here. The target lacks sufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables within Defense & Security to support a reliable median multiple. A search across stage and valuation tier filtered the initial 21-peer universe to zero qualifying matches, falling short of the minimum three-datapoint threshold required for statistical validity. Applying an all-sector median would introduce material bias given the heterogeneity of Defense & Security business models, margins, and growth profiles.

Given these constraints, valuation rests on qualitative positioning relative to the identified peer set rather than quantitative triangulation. Public comps offer limited guidance due to structural differences between public-market incumbents and private-stage frontier-tech operators. Private peers span diverse sub-verticals and financing stages, limiting their direct applicability. The $250M+ raised floor provides a concrete reference point but does not itself determine fair value. Absent sufficient narrow-peer data, key uncertainties center on revenue trajectory, unit economics relative to scaled Defense & Security players, and the sustainability of private-market exit multiples in this sector.
